---
title: "Thinking Across Scales"
subtitle: "An unfinished manifesto"
date: 2026-07-07

format:
  html:
    page-layout: article
 
categories:
  - Philosophy
  - Ecology
  - Systems Thinking
---
> Every landscape is also a way of thinking.

We often explain problems at the scale of individuals, when their roots lie in larger systems. We debate policies at the national level, yet rarely ask how they are lived by a single person. Thinking Across Scales is the practice of moving continuously between these perspectives—between individuals and institutions, places and planets, moments and millennia. Ecological problems are fundamentally problems of scale. I have come to believe that life is, too.

I did not arrive at this idea through philosophy.

I arrived through fieldwork.

Years of ecological investigations across China's forests, wetlands, grasslands, and mountains slowly revealed a recurring pattern.

The questions I carried into the field were rarely the questions I brought home.

A fallen tulip tree became a story about institutions.
A cigarette butt became a story about policy.
A wetland became a story about time.

The Ecology of the Mind is my attempt to continue these conversations.

It is not a travel blog.

It is not an academic website.

It is a living collection of journeys, essays, conversations, and ideas exploring how landscapes shape the ways we think—and how our ways of thinking, in turn, reshape the landscapes we inhabit.

This is where the journey begins.
